title: Ensure that the --root-ca-file argument is set as appropriate

input_row.title: Ensure that the --root-ca-file argument is set as appropriate
input_row.description: Allow pods to verify the API server's serving certificate before establishing connections.
input_row.rationale: Processes running within pods that need to contact the API server must verify the API server's serving certificate. Failing to do so could be a subject to man-in-the-middle attacks. Providing the root certificate for the API server's serving certificate to the controller manager with the --root-ca-file argument allows the controller manager to inject the trusted bundle into pods so that they can verify TLS connections to the API server.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep kube-controller-manager Verify that the --root-ca-file argument exists and is set to a certificate bundle file containing the root certificate for the API server's serving certificate.
input_row.remediation: Edit the /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager file on the master node and set the KUBE_CONTROLLER_MANAGER_ARGS parameter to include --root-ca-file=<file>: KUBE_CONTROLLER_MANAGER_ARGS="--root-ca-file=<file>" Based on your system, restart the kube-controller-manager service. For example: systemctl restart kube-controller-manager.service Impact: You need to setup and maintain root certificate authority file. Default Value: By default, --root-ca-file is not set  87 | P a g e References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-controller-manager/ 2. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/11000
input_row.impact: You need to setup and maintain root certificate authority file. Default Value: By default, --root-ca-file is not set  87 | P a g e

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires verification that the kube-controller-manager process is started with the --root-ca-file argument set to a valid certificate bundle file. The audit procedure in the CIS benchmark specifies running a process listing (ps -ef | grep kube-controller-manager) on the master node and visually inspecting the process arguments. This information is not exposed via the Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any standard Kubernetes resource; it is only available at the host OS level where the controller manager runs. Therefore, this check cannot be fully automated using Kubernetes APIs or kubectl and requires manual inspection of the process or configuration file on the host.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into the master node(s) where the kube-controller-manager is running.
2. Run the following command to list the kube-controller-manager process and its arguments:
   ```
   ps -ef | grep kube-controller-manager
   ```
3. Inspect the output to verify that the --root-ca-file argument is present and points to a valid certificate bundle file (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt).
4. Optionally, verify the contents of the referenced file to ensure it contains the correct root certificate for the API server's serving certificate.
5. Confirm compliance if the --root-ca-file argument is set and references a valid certificate bundle file. If the argument is missing or incorrectly set, this is non-compliant.
